title: Multiple-choice cloze
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: Modern neuroscience has revealed surprising insights into how the brain processes creativity. Early models (0) assumed that creative thinking was confined to a single region, but recent findings show it involves a complex network of interacting systems.||One key discovery is that the brain must (1) between spontaneous idea generation and deliberate evaluation. When this balance is disrupted, individuals may struggle to (2) their thoughts into coherent outcomes.||Researchers also highlight the importance of mental rest, noting that creativity often emerges when people temporarily (3) from demanding tasks. This downtime allows the mind to (4) unexpected connections that might otherwise remain hidden.||To cultivate creativity, experts recommend practices that (5) curiosity and reduce performance pressure. However, they warn that society tends to (6) originality in favour of efficiency, which can (7) the development of innovative thinking. Ultimately, the challenge is to (8) environments where imagination can truly flourish.

example.explanation: “Assumed” is the correct academic verb for describing outdated scientific models. The others are informal or vague.

questions.explanation: “Switch between” is the natural collocation. The others are possible but less idiomatic in cognitive science contexts.
“Channel thoughts into outcomes” is idiomatic. The others imply physical direction or shaping.
“Step back from tasks” is the fixed phrase. The others shift the meaning toward emotional withdrawal or aimlessness.
“Spark connections” is idiomatic. The others imply physical creation or extraction.
“Nurture curiosity” is the standard collocation. The others are possible but less precise.
“Downplay originality” is idiomatic. The others imply dominance or avoidance.
“Hamper development” is the correct collocation. The others do not fit the context.
“Foster environments” is idiomatic. The others imply fixing something in place, which does not fit the metaphor.
